posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
EUROPEAN 2003S - Chicago, IL (7/28/2016)

  • 2003 Trimbach Riesling Clos Ste. Hune - France, Alsace
    A rather fat and large scaled expression of riesling. This is a powerful and muscular wine. The nose shows a fair bit of herbaceousness as well. On the palate, this wine is somehow sweet but without residual sugar. There’s acidity, but the odd thing is that it isn’t the driving force behind the wine (especially weird in riesling). The fruit profile here is quite tropical, with some pineapple notes. Perhaps more interesting than good, at least, to my palate. (88 pts.)
  • 2003 Gérard Raphet Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Lavaux St. Jacques Cuvée Unique - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru
    The nose here shows a fair bit of rustic barnyard, and beneath it, you find some interesting whiffs of red fruit. The palate shows quite sweet, although there’s a touch of acidity here as well. While this wine isn’t hot, the alcohol does stick out in a very disjointed way. The fruit shows ripeness, but not overly so. This completely fell apart with air. (85 pts.)
  • 2003 Elio Altare Barolo Vigneto Arborina - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo
    Perhaps my objection to this wine is that it gives itself away much too easily. I prefer to grapple with my Barolos. There’s a light touch of toast on the nose, but the palate is just super silky. Very pleasing red fruit, this wine is extremely pliant and soft. Not my style of Barolo, but I’ll admit it did make for some pleasurable drinking, with its ripe red fruit and missing tannins. Hardly a challenging wine, I got bored of it pretty quickly. (85 pts.)
  • 2003 Giacomo Conterno Barolo Cascina Francia - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo
    This is a brutally monolithic expression of Barolo, far more pleasing to me by virtue of its taciturn nature than the extremely giving Altare Arborina. There’s hardly any fruit here. It’s all dirt, minerals, and tannin. And somehow at the same time, it’s so very clear there’s something really good going on here. It’s a wine that requires time to explore, even though it’s so brutally big. One to cellar – this is as serious a Barolo as they come. (90 pts.)
  • 2003 Pago de Carraovejas Ribera del Duero Reserva - Spain, Castilla y León, Ribera del Duero
    Really not my thing. It’s that international toasted oak, blueberry milkshake kind of wine. Since I’ve never had this wine before, I can’t tell if it’s a victim of the vintage or if that’s what the wine is aiming for. In either case, pass. (70 pts.)
  • 2003 Canalicchio di Sopra Brunello di Montalcino - Italy, Tuscany, Montalcino, Brunello di Montalcino
    A horrid, stewey heap of some unidentifiable organic matter. It’s like it’s so ripe it’s turned rotten. Into the dump bucket. (70 pts.)
  • 2003 Château Montrose - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Estèphe
    A brilliant Montrose. This is currently better than the 2005. It shows plenty of that Montrosian earthiness, coupled with some approachable fruit as well, rendering this a much more balanced drink than the 2005 (which is shut down and full of nothing but earth at the moment). There are nascent secondary characteristics developing, and I’d be optimistic about the future of this wine. While it’s enjoyable now thanks to the fruit balancing out the stereotypically sterner nature of Montrose, this is certainly a great candidate for aging. (90 pts.)
  • 2003 Château Chauvin - France, Bordeaux, Libournais, St. Émilion Grand Cru
    Rive droite rôtie? Roasted fruit, roasted oak. Throw in some roasting from the alcoholic burn too. In the words of Lana Kane, from the animated show Archer, “Nooooooooooooope.” (70 pts.)
  • 2003 Domaine de Bonserine Côte-Rôtie La Sarrasine - France, Rhône, Northern Rhône, Côte-Rôtie
    While all the elements of a good syrah are there, this is just all put together in the wrong way, like Frankenstein’s monster if good Doctor Frankenstein were blind. Bizarrely, this seems to be an acid-driven wine, which totally doesn’t jive with the ripe fruit flavours. There’s a touch of meat and olive, but only barely. Somehow it seems this was simultaneously picked too early and too late. (83 pts.)
  • 2003 Weingut Reinhold Haart Piesporter Goldtröpfchen Riesling Auslese Goldkapsel Auction - Germany, Mosel Saar Ruwer
    #17-04, 7.5% abv. Brilliant riesling. I’m starting to see the light with respect to this vintage in Germany, and the light is good. This is a massive and monumental example of an Auslese, showing lots of sweetness, as well as plenty of botrytis-inflected flavours. Good spiciness, and tasty lychee notes. Very, very sweet, and yet, while there might not be a super amount of acid, this still feels very much in balance. I’d be curious to see where this goes with age. (93 pts.)
  • 2003 Fritz Haag Brauneberger Juffer-Sonnenuhr Riesling Auslese Goldkapsel #9 - Germany, Mosel Saar Ruwer
    #09-04, 7.5% abv. This bottle showed a lot of cheesy funk, which I actually found a little bit disconcerting, but it did blow off with air to reveal a very cool expression of riesling. No signs of overripeness to be found at all, this is a very judiciously put-together bottle of wine. The fruit, in fact, isn’t too sweet at all, and there’s some acidic lift to this wine at the same time. There are some clear signs of botrytis. (90 pts.)
  • 2003 Domaine Huet Vouvray Moelleux Le Haut-Lieu - France, Loire Valley, Touraine, Vouvray
    Served blind (except that we knew it was a 2003). Immediately identifiable as chenin thanks to the furry elements on the nose. The palate is only moderately sweet, and shows some alcoholic disjointedness as well. I guessed a demi-sec Loire – and was sorely disappointed when this was revealed. There’s no way this is anywhere close to sweet enough that I would consider it a Moelleux, and given how poorly put-together this is, I’m shocked that Huet could put their good name on the bottle. (83 pts.)
  • 2003 Barbeito Madeira Tinta Negra Mole Single Harvest Colheita - Portugal, Madeira
    3416/4004, bottled April 2014. From half-litre. Oh to be a victim of being romanced at the winery, only to buy a bottle that doesn’t show as well when you bring it home (for indeed, this was purchased at the Lodge and hand-carried back home). A very light bottle of madeira indeed, this was just slightly darker than the Haag BJS GK Auslese. Touch of nuttiness and citrus, this had that lighter feel that I liked in the newer Barbeito wines, but it didn’t quite have the same depth and concentration that I remembered. Quite short, as a matter of fact. (85 pts.)

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: Talk about taking 1 (or 2 or 3 or…) for the team! Better you than us - thank you!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hard to disagree, though I might have scored the Montrose more generously than you.

Good to see someone else seeing the light re. Mosel 2003s. The Prüm wines are lovely too, as are Willi Schaefer’s.

And I also often wonder about the yeastiness of young Haag.

Thanks for posting.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked: For my palate, 2003 Montrose is one of the best wines ever produced by the estate! I have given it 99/100 Pts on several occasions.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
I agree with Jeff. Had the 2003 Montrose only twice and it´s a superb wine IMO. As many 2003 Bordeaux are. Sure – some are actually awful but I even love some Right Bank wines. 2003 is a very mixed case indeed. But Burgundy did produce some superb wines also. 2003 Clos de Tart is wonderful IMO. I am not the biggest CdP fan. But 2003 Pegeau is really good and it does´t taste as a monster.

My suggestion: Don´t show the vintage to people when you pour 2003. Many will be surprised that the good versions don´t taste like prune juice.

But to everyone his one.

Jürgen Steinke:

2003 is a very mixed case indeed. But Burgundy did produce some superb wines also. 2003 Clos de Tart is wonderful IMO.

I thought Clos de Tart and Clos des Lambrays both did quite well in 03.

posts.name: Blake R Maso
posts.cooked: Montrose 03 is an utterly compelling wine with a singular voice. Im personally of the belief that the few tops of 03 BDX are above the tops of 05, and this is one of the tops for sure, it still has a ways to go and is a kaleidoscopic skyscraper of a wine touching if not embodying vinous perfection… But I like this style and it definitely isn’t for everyone… I think 03s should all be blind affairs.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked: Gonna echo what’s already been said about 2003 Mosel Riesling. It’s a very good vintage for Auslese.

posts.name: Vincent Fritzsche
posts.cooked: Moelleux is not necessarily a sweet wine. I haven’t had '03 from Huet but if you’ve ever had Foreau’s basic Moelleux from any vintage, it’s rarely very sweet at all. I used to think - that’s not right. But turns out it is. 1er true is another story.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Dennis Kanagie:

Gonna echo what’s already been said about 2003 Mosel Riesling. It’s a very good vintage for Auslese.

+1

posts.name: Leonard Maran
posts.cooked: I’d just add that the 90 point Montrose is a “brilliant Montrose” is a little hard for me to understand.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Leonard Maran:

I’d just add that the 90 point Montrose is a “brilliant Montrose” is a little hard for me to understand.

Why? On CellarTracker the scoring system shows 90 - 93 as Excellent. Looks like our OP isn’t using the new inflated scoring system used by Parker and Suckling.

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked: I’m also a big fan of the 2003 Château Montrose - a fantastic wine with great balance. I also find the 90 point score on the low side. 2003 Huets are generally quite good, but I never had the Le Haut Lieu Moelleux I’d second what Vincent Fritzsche said: Huet Moelleux do not taste very sweet, I think they usually have something like 50-60 g/l of residual sugar, but quite a lot of dry extract, so that the sweetness is hidden.

posts.name: Keith A k e r s
posts.cooked:
Leonard Maran:

I’d just add that the 90 point Montrose is a “brilliant Montrose” is a little hard for me to understand.

Or that Adrian can see the inherent quality in the wine, but that on the night it was grumpy.

On the whole, why are people so up in arms over arbitrary scores? Does Adrian scoring the 03 Montrose (and it’s a reflection of where that specific bottle was on that specific night) take away from other people’s enjoyment of it? If that is the case, then maybe one should be asking themselves why it matters that much
posts.display_username: Keith A k e r s

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Keith A k e r s:

On the whole, why are people so up in arms over arbitrary scores? Does Adrian scoring the 03 Montrose (and it’s a reflection of where that specific bottle was on that specific night) take away from other people’s enjoyment of it? If that is the case, then maybe one should be asking themselves why it matters that much

Keith… Are you saying people should not comment if they do not agree? Plus, I think everyone was rather polite. Not one person was up in arms. The OP has his own taste, which is fine. That’s how he sees it. But others do not agree, which is also fine. If people only responded when they agreed, there would be a lot less exchanges online.

I like reading differing views. When I know what wines the posters like or not over time, it helps me decide how much weight I want to give their tasting notes in either direction.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Jeff Leve:

Plus, I think everyone was rather polite. Not one person was up in arms.

Agreed - this is a surpringly polite discussion for being Berserkers.

… so far.

posts.name: Keith A k e r s
posts.cooked:
Jeff,

I think people are looking at the score only and not truly reading the text. It’s clear that there was enjoyment, so what does the actual number given to it truly matter? I was at the dinner too and found that the wine opened was in an odd spot at the moment. Does that mean it’s not a possibly great wine? Absolutely not. Does that mean that the wine that was open wasn’t great on that exact moment and on that exact night, yes. The actual number is rather superfluous to saying that the quality was there, but maybe not entirely showing itself in that moment.
posts.display_username: Keith A k e r s

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Keith A k e r s:

Jeff,

I think people are looking at the score only and not truly reading the text. It’s clear that there was enjoyment, so what does the actual number given to it truly matter? I was at the dinner too and found that the wine opened was in an odd spot at the moment. Does that mean it’s not a possibly great wine? Absolutely not. Does that mean that the wine that was open wasn’t great on that exact moment and on that exact night, yes. The actual number is rather superfluous to saying that the quality was there, but maybe not entirely showing itself in that moment.

Keith… You’re sticking up for the posters note as if he’s correct, and all the other posters are wrong. Both have their own views and they differ. Why is that an issue?

As for the scores, if scores do not matter, people should not score wines. But scores do matter, (as it says something about the wine, and the writers views) and so does the note attached. FWIW, I do not agree with the text on the note either I find 2003 Montrose extraordinary to taste today! My most recent note was from May 2016 of this year. Learn about Chateau Montrose St. Estephe, Complete Guide

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked: Perhaps being of Commonwealth descent, I prefer my claret a little more… aged.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
A. So:

Perhaps being of Commonwealth descent, I prefer my claret a little more… aged.

That’s it. I’m selling my 03 Montrose. Until it’s aged and then you score it right, Mr. So So!
